risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients who met the inclusion criteria were randomly assigned to one of two groups" Comment: The paper states that participants were randomly assigned, but it does not describe how the allocation sequence was generated (e.g., computer-generated list, random number table, blocks, drawing lots). With no further method details or context clues confirming a truly random sequence, the risk is unclear rather than high.

Quote: "Patients who met the inclusion criteria were randomly assigned to one of two groups" Comment: The article states that participants were randomly assigned, but it does not describe any allocation concealment mechanism. There is no explicit report of central randomization, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, pharmacy-controlled allocation, or any other procedure that would prevent the person enrolling participants from foreseeing the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Neither group was aware of the treatment that the other group was receiving." and "The subjects in the experimental group performed... exercises... The control group received ultrasound therapy... They were told to continue their normal daily activities, and no extra exercises were applied." Comment: This does not indicate blinding of participants or treating personnel to each participant's own assignment; rather, the intervention descriptions show an open comparison (exercise program plus ultrasound vs ultrasound/no extra exercises), so participants and personnel delivering treatment would be aware of allocation. Because key outcomes included pain intensity (NRS) and function (WOMAC), which are subjective and plausibly influenced by knowledge of treatment received, performance bias is high.

Quote: "Neither group was aware of the treatment that the other group was receiving. All measurements of dependent variables were obtained by another therapist who was blinded to group assignment." and "The outcome measures or dependent variables selected for this study were pain intensity, knee function, and isometric quadriceps strength. These variables were measured using the Numerical Rating Scale (NRS), a reduced WOMAC index, and a strength gauge device, respectively." Comment: Although a therapist blinded to group assignment measured some outcomes, the study also included subjective patient-reported outcomes (pain NRS and WOMAC function). The statement indicates participants were only unaware of the other group's treatment, not blinded to their own intervention; given one group performed exercises and the other did not, participants were likely aware of their allocation. For self-reported pain/function, the patient is the outcome assessor, so lack of participant blinding makes these outcomes vulnerable to detection bias. By the worst-outcome rule, the presence of unblinded subjective outcomes makes the overall domain High risk.

Summary: The paper reports that 42 subjects were enrolled and randomized, with 21 in each group: "Forty-two subjects were enrolled in the study, with 21 subjects divided in each group." Outcomes were measured at baseline and week 5, but the article does not explicitly state how many participants in each arm contributed outcome data at week 5, does not report any post-randomization withdrawals/losses/exclusions, and the referenced CONSORT diagram is not available in the supplied text. Comment: Because per-arm analysed numbers and attrition reasons for the analysed outcomes are not directly reported, post-randomization missing outcome data cannot be quantified; therefore this domain is judged unclear rather than high.
